region_of_origin: Europe and Western Asia (widely cultivated globally)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fresh cherries is approximately 138 g (about 1 cup pitted or roughly 21 cherries), providing around 69 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Cherries, sweet, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 09070)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, dietary fibre, sodium) are sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database (data_source: hpb, confidence 0.95) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for sweet cherries (FDC ID 09070). Extended micronutrients (saturated fat, sugar, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin B6, folate, magnesium, phosphorus, zinc) are drawn from USDA SR Legacy. Values are consistent between sources.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sweet Cherries — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 22 for sweet cherries (Prunus avium) is directly reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of GI Values. GL per serving calculated as: GI (22) × carbohydrate per serving (138 g × 12.18 g/100 g = 16.81 g) / 100 = 3.70, rounded to 3.7. Reported as 3.0 using a conservative 10-cherry (80 g) reference portion: 22 × (80 × 12.18/100) / 100 = 22 × 9.74 / 100 = 2.14 ≈ 2.1; using standard 138 g cup serving GL ≈ 3.7. Value set to 3.7 in final record.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (3 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium (173 mg/100 g) supporting a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary guidelines
hypertension.guidance: Cherries are suitable for individuals with hypertension; their negligible sodium content and moderate potassium level (173 mg/100 g) support blood pressure management, and a standard serving of ~138 g contributes approximately 239 mg potassium with only 4 mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycaemic index (GI 22) with natural sugars buffered by dietary fibre (1.6 g/100 g), limiting postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cherries have a low GI of 22 and a glycaemic load of approximately 2–4 per serving, making them appropriate for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes when consumed in moderate portions (10–15 cherries); whole fresh cherries are preferred over juice or syrup-preserved forms.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in anthocyanins and polyphenols with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties; low saturated fat (0.07 g/100 g) and zero trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cherries are cardioprotective due to their high anthocyanin content, which has been associated with reduced LDL oxidation and lower inflammatory markers (CRP); their negligible saturated fat and absence of trans fat make them a heart-healthy fruit choice within a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High anthocyanin and polyphenol content with documented anti-inflammatory effects; clinical evidence for reduction of CRP and uric acid
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Regular consumption of cherries, particularly tart varieties, has been shown in clinical studies to reduce serum uric acid and CRP levels relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; fresh sweet cherries also provide vitamin C (~7 mg/100 g) as an additional antioxidant.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cherry (Prunus avium) is a small, round stone fruit with smooth, glossy deep-red to dark-purple skin and sweet, juicy red flesh surrounding a single hard pit. Fresh sweet cherries are consumed raw as a snack or dessert fruit and are widely available in temperate regions globally. They have a characteristic sweet-tart flavour with a firm yet yielding texture. Nutritionally, cherries are a low-calorie fruit (50 kcal/100 g) with moderate carbohydrates (12.18 g/100 g), low fat (0.3 g/100 g), and modest protein (1.0 g/100 g). Their dietary fibre content (1.6 g/100 g) helps moderate sugar absorption. Cherries have a low glycaemic index of 22, making them suitable for blood glucose management. They are rich in anthocyanins and polyphenols, which confer anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties relevant to cardiovascular health and inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. Potassium content (173 mg/100 g) supports blood pressure regulation consistent with DASH dietary principles. Cherries are naturally free of gluten, dairy, and common allergens, and are suitable for vegan, vegetarian, halal, and kosher diets. They are best consumed fresh and in season, though also available frozen, dried, or as juice. Clinically, moderate portions (10–15 cherries, ~80–100 g) are recommended for individuals managing diabetes or weight.
common_user_queries: What is the glycemic index of cherries?
Can I eat cherries if I have high blood pressure?
What are the health benefits of cherries?
How much potassium is in cherries?
Are cherries suitable for a vegan diet?
What is the serving size for cherries?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database (confidence 0.95, reviewed) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 09070) for sweet cherries. GI value of 22 is directly reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables. Extended micronutrients drawn from USDA SR Legacy. All values are internally consistent and supported by two independent approved sources.
notes: cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): cherries are a plant food and naturally contain no cholesterol per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 09070). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): plant foods do not synthesise vitamin B12. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy). selenium_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed trace/zero per USDA SR Legacy). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (naturally zero in fresh fruit). GL per serving recalculated: using 138 g cup serving, GL = 22 × (138 × 0.1218) / 100 = 22 × 16.81 / 100 = 3.70; value set to 3.7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g values sourced from USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 09070). No mandatory multilingual names required as cherry is a globally generic fruit not classified under any single national cuisine requiring G6 language coverage. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code are defensible best-match entries; FoodEx2 code A00LC corresponds to the 'Cherries' node in the EFSA FoodEx2 classification system.
